Polyprotic Acid-Base Equilibria Diprotic Acids and Bases
Section 10-1 Diprotic Acids and Bases
Section 10-2 Diprotic Buffers
Section 10-3 Polyprotic Acids and Bases

Diprotic Systems (Have Two Acidic or Basic Sites)
Amino acids: 20 chemical building blocks that make up proteins. Each has:
• A n acidic carboxylic acid group •  A basic amino group • A variable substituent “R”
Zwitterion has both positive and negative sites
Carbonyl group is stronger than ammonium group
Nonionized form rearranges spontaneously to the zwitterion

Amino Acids
At low pH, both the ammonium group and the carbonyl group are protonated.
At high pH, neither group is protonated.
Acid dissociation constants of amino acids can be found in Appendix G ( each compound is drawn in its fully protonated form ).
Zwitterions in solution are stabilized by interactions of
Figure 10-2

Amino Acid Leucine (HL)
R = isobutyl group (CH 3 ) 2 CHCH 2 
Diprotic acid: H 2 L +
HL + H + K a1 ≡ K 1
H L
L − + H + K a2 ≡ K 2
Diprotic base: L − + H 2 O
H L + OH − K b1
HL + H 2 O
H 2 L + + OH − K b2
Relations between K a and K b : K a1 ∙ K b2 = K W
K a2 ∙ K b1 = K W
Table 10-1 Acid dissociation constants of amino acids from Appendix G (1 of 3)
Table 10-1 Acid dissociation constants of amino acids from Appendix G (2 of 3)

Table 10-1 Acid dissociation constants of amino acids from Appendix G (3 of 3)
The acidic protons are shown in bold type. Each amino acid is written in its fully protonated form. Standard abbreviations are shown in parentheses.
p K a values refer to 25°C and zero ionic strength unless marked by c. Values considered to be uncertain are enclosed in parentheses. Appendix G gives pK a for μ = 0.1 M .
For these entries, the ionic strength is 0.1 M, and the constant refers to a product of concentrations instead of activities.

Example: Interpreting Amino Acid Structures in Appendix G (1 of 3)
Chemical structures in Appendix G are drawn fully protonated. The name of each compound refers to the neutral molecule with no net charge. Leucine is drawn as
This is not neutral leucine. This structure has a charge of +1.
This structure could be abbreviated H 2 L + , where L is for leucine.
The chemistry of H 2 L + is the same as for any diprotic acid H 2 A.
which is not the neutral compound. Two acidic protons are shown in bold color. Values of K a listed in Appendix G are 2.328 (CO 2 H) and 9.744 (NH 3 ).

Example: Interpreting Amino Acid Structures in Appendix G (2 of 3)
The larger value K a = 10 −2.328 is for the carboxyl proton, so the structure when one proton is lost is
This is leucine with a net charge of 0.
This structure could be abbreviated HL, where L is for leucine.
The chemistry of HL is the same as for the intermediate form of any diprotic acid HA − .
The smaller value K a = 10 −9.744 is for the ammonium proton, so the structure when the second proton is lost is
This structure could be abbreviated L − , where L is for leucine.
The chemistry of L − is the same as for the fully basic form A 2− of any diprotic acid.

Diprotic Acids and Bases
There are three forms of diprotic amino acids.
H2L+ | fully protonated form | behaves same as H2A
HL | intermediate form | behaves same as  HA
L− | fully basic form | behaves same as diprotic A2
How do we calculate the pH and composition of individual solutions of H 2 L + , HL, and L − ?
Use the same procedure to find the pH of diprotic H 2 A (where A is anything).
E.g., H 2 L + where HL is leucine

Acidic Form, 0.050 0 M H 2 L +
H 2 L + can dissociate twice.
H 2 L + is a weak acid ( K 1 = 4.70 × 10 −3 ); it will dissociate only partly.
HL is an even weaker acid ( K 2 = 1.80 × 10 −10 ); it will hardly dissociate at all.
Approximation: H 2 L + behaves as a monoprotic acid with K a = K 1 .
[HL] = x = 1.32 × 10 −2 M
[H 2 L + ] = F − x = 0.050 0 − 1.32 × 10 −2 = 3.68 × 10 −2 M
pH = 1.88

Basic Form, 0.050 0 M L −
L − (found in a salt such as NaL ) can undergo two hydrolysis reactions.
L − is a weak base ( K b1 = K w / K a2 =5.55 × 10 −5 ); it will not hydrolyze very much.
HL is an even weaker base ( K b2 = K w / K a1 =2.13 × 10 −12 ); it will hardly react with water at all, making very little H 2 L + .
Approximation: L − behaves as a monobasic system with K b = K b1 .
[HL] = x = 1.64 × 10 −3 M
[L − ] = F − x = 0.050 0 − 1.64 × 10 −3 = 4.84 × 10 −2 M
pH = 11.21

Intermediate Form, HL (Amphiprotic)
A solution made from leucine (HL) is more complicated because HL is both an acid and a base ( amphiprotic ).
HL
H + + L − K a = K a2 = 1.80 × 10 −10
HL + H 2 O
H 2 L + + OH − K b = K b2 = 2.13 × 10 −12
Since the acid dissociation reaction has a larger equilibrium constant ( K a2 ), expect the solution to be acidic .
But we cannot ignore the hydrolysis reaction ( K b2 ).
Both reactions proceed to some extent.
Method: Resort to the systematic treatment of equilibrium.

Intermediate Form, 0.050 0 M HL (Amphiprotic) (1 of 2)
Mathematical solution for calculating [H + ] in solution is derived using the systematic treatment of equilibrium
(10-10)
HL is the dominant species because it is both a weak acid and a weak base, so the equation becomes
(10-11)
If F = 0.050 0 M
pH = 6.06

Intermediate Form, 0.050 0 M HL (Amphiprotic) (2 of 2)
The concentration of H 2 L + is found from the K 1 expression.
The concentration of L − is found from the K 2 expression.
[HL] = F = 0.050 0 M

Summary of Results for Leucine
Notice the relative concentrations of H 2 L + , HL, and L − in each solution, and the pH trend.

Box 10-2 Successive Approximations (1 of 2)
Successive approximations is a good way to deal with difficult equations that do not have simple solutions.
Equation 10-11 is not a good approximation when the concentration of the intermediate species of a diprotic acid is not close to F. This situation arises when K 1 and K 2 are nearly equal and F is small.
By estimating one value needed to complete a calculation and critically looking at the result, it is possible to repeat the calculation with better estimates.
With successive attempts, it is possible to hone in on the correct value.

Simplified Calculations for the Intermediate Form
A simpler form of the calculation results if two common conditions are met:
•  If K 2 F   >> K w •  If K 1 <<  F
In this case, several terms in Equation 10-11 cancel, including the formal concentration (F), leaving
The pH of the intermediate form of a diprotic acid is close to midway between p K 1 and p K 2 , regardless of the formal concentration .

Example: pH of the Intermediate Form of a Diprotic Acid (1 of 3)
Potassium hydrogen phthalate, KHP, is a salt of the intermediate form of phthalic acid. Calculate the pH of 0.10 M and 0.010 M KHP.

Solution: With Equation 10-12, the pH of potassium hydrogen phthalate is estimated as
regardless of concentration. With Equation 10-11, we calculate pH = 4.18 for 0.10 M K + HP − and pH = 4.20 for 0.010 M K + HP − .

Diprotic Buffers
A buffer made from a diprotic (or polyprotic) acid is treated in the same way as a buffer made from a monoprotic acid.
We can write two Henderson-Hasselbalch equations, both of which are always true for a solution at equilibrium.

Example: A Diprotic Buffer System (1 of 3)
Find the pH of a solution prepared by dissolving 1.00 g of potassium hydrogen phthalate and 1.20 g of disodium phthalate in 50.0 mL of water.

Example: A Diprotic Buffer System (2 of 3)
Solution: Monohydrogen phthalate and phthalate were shown in the preceding example. The formula masses are KHP = C 8 H 5 O 4 K = 204.22 and Na 2 P = C 8 H 4 O 4 Na 2 = 210.10. We know [HP − ] and [P 2− ], so we use the p K 2 Henderson-Hasselbalch equation to find the pH:
K 2 is the acid dissociation constant of HP − , which appears in the denominator of the log term. The volume of solution was not needed to answer the question.

Example: Preparing a Buffer in a Diprotic System (1 of 4)
How many milliliters of 0.800 M KOH should be added to 3.38 g of oxalic acid to give a pH of 4.40 when diluted to 500 mL?
p K 1 = 1.250
p K 2 = 4.266

Example: Preparing a Buffer in a Diprotic System (2 of 4)
Solution: The desired pH is above p K 2 . We know that a 1:1 mole ratio of HOx − : Ox 2− would have pH = p K 2 = 4.266. If the pH is to be 4.40, there must be more Ox 2− than HOx − present. We must add enough base to convert all H 2 Ox into HOx − , plus enough additional base to convert the right amount of HOx − into Ox 2− .
In 3.38 g of H 2 Ox, there are 0.037 5 4 mol. The volume of 0.800 M KOH needed to react with this much H 2 Ox to make HOx − is (0.037 5 4 mol)/(0.800 M) = 46.9 3 mL.

Example: Preparing a Buffer in a Diprotic System (3 of 4)
Solution: To produce a pH of 4.40 requires an additional x mol of OH − :
The volume of KOH needed to deliver 0.021 6 6 mole is (0.021 6 4 mol)/(0.800 M) = 27.0 5 mL. The total volume of KOH needed to bring the pH to 4.40 is 46.9 3 + 27.0 5 = 73.9 8 mL.

Polyprotic Acids and Bases (1 of 2)
The treatment of diprotic acids and bases can be extended to polyprotic systems. Consider a triprotic system:
H 3 A
H 2 A − + H + K a1 = K 1
H 2 A −
HA 2− + H + K a2 = K 2
HA 2−
A 3− + H + K a3 = K 3
A 3− + H 2 O
HA 2− + OH − K b1 = K W / K a3
HA 2− + H 2 O
H 2 A − + OH − K b2 = K W / K a2
H 2 A − + H 2 O
H 3 A + OH − K b3 = K W / K a1

Polyprotic Acids and Bases (2 of 2)
The acid-base problems can be reduced to just three types.
Decide if you are dealing with an acidic , basic , or intermediate form . Then do the appropriate arithmetic. For a triprotic acid:
H 3 A is treated as a monoprotic weak acid, with K a = K 1 .
H 2 A − is treated as an intermediate form of a diprotic acid ( K 1 and K 2 ).
HA 2− is treated as an intermediate form of a diprotic acid ( K 2 and K 3 ).
A 3− is treated as monobasic, with K b = K b1 = K w / K a3 .

Example: A Triprotic System (1 of 6)
Find the pH for each of the solutions 0.10 M H 3 His 2+ , 0.10 M H 2 His + , 0.10 M HHis , and 0.10 M His − , where His stands for the amino acid histidine.
